title: AGY-102: The UI Landscape & Selection (L100)
description: This course provides a comprehensive comparative tour of Google Antigravity's three main command interfaces: the standalone visual board (antigravity), the lightweight command line interface (agy), and the agent-integrated workspace editor (antigravity-ide). Learners will explore the design philosophy, target use cases, configuration setups, and selection workflows for each UI surface, culminating in a clear understanding of how to seamlessly coordinate across all three for peak agentic productivity.

highlights.title: Comparative Interface Tour
highlights.description: Master the functional differences between the CLI, Standalone UI, and IDE integration.

highlights.title: Practical CLI Pipes & Scripts
highlights.description: Learn to execute high-velocity batch tasks, folder queries, and CLI integration workflows using the native agy command line.

highlights.title: Cross-Interface Interoperability
highlights.description: Understand how the three interfaces synchronize under the hood to enable seamless workflow transitions.

blocks.content: Understanding the Decoupled Paradigm

Google Antigravity introduces an agentic engineering model that cleanly decouples backend task execution from frontend interaction surfaces. Traditional IDEs and coding assistants force developers into a single, synchronous chat or autocomplete interface, limiting productivity and flexibility. Antigravity resolves this technical friction by running its execution engine on three distinct surfaces: a local script-based environment, a standalone secure tarball, or a containerized sandbox (agy-box). Above this layer, developers can choose between three distinct frontend command surfaces depending on their operational context, role, and current goal.

Design Principle: Decoupling ensures that UI preferences do not affect the engine's core capabilities. Whether you direct your agent visually or script it in a shell, the engine enforces the same workspace boundary locks, directory permissions, credential storage, and security rules.
Separation of Concerns: Agent Manager vs. Editor

In Antigravity, the user experience is divided into two primary workspaces to mirror the natural division of labor in software engineering: high-level task management and low-level code implementation.

items: Agent Manager (Mission Control): Designed for high-level asynchronous orchestration. Users can define high-level objectives, spawn parallel agent instances to work on multiple tasks or bugs simultaneously, manage credentials, and review generated artifacts.

Editor Interface: Designed for low-level synchronous co-authoring and editing. It retains the familiar, low-latency editing features of Visual Studio Code (file explorers, syntax highlighting, language extensions) while introducing inline code generation tools.

blocks.content: Agent Execution Modes: Fast vs. Planning
To control the agent's level of reasoning and resource consumption, users can toggle between two primary execution modes in the command interfaces:

items: Fast Mode (Standard Execution): The agent executes tasks directly with minimal thinking latency and resource consumption. Best suited for simple, localized modifications such as variable renaming, execution of straightforward bash commands, or minor edits where review is unnecessary.

Planning Mode (Deep Reasoning): The agent constructs an explicit implementation plan and a sequential task list before modifying files. It works in structured task groups, produces detailed artifacts, and stops for human verification at critical gates. Best suited for complex refactorings, multi-step web research, or new feature additions.

blocks.content: Interface Selection Guidelines
Each surface is tailored to specific workloads and environments:

items: Use the CLI Client (agy) for rapid terminal checks, scripting batch updates, piping outputs, and running headless container environments.
Use the Standalone Visual Board (antigravity) for parallel asynchronous workflows, monitoring multi-agent pipelines, tracking visual web browsing, and inspecting playback tapes.
Use the Workspace Editor (antigravity-ide) for active coding sessions, inline natural language updates (Cmd+I), line-by-line diff reviews, and co-authoring document structures.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the primary difference between the Agent Manager and the Editor workspaces?
- A) The Agent Manager executes compiled languages, while the Editor runs interpreted scripting languages.
- B) The Agent Manager is designed for asynchronous, multi-agent orchestration, while the Editor is designed for synchronous, side-by-side file editing and code review.
- C) The Agent Manager only runs inside containers, whereas the Editor must be installed natively on the host workstation.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Antigravity splits the interface to allow high-level asynchronous delegation in the Agent Manager, and synchronous, hands-on code adjustments in the Editor.
Question 2: Under what conditions should you select Planning Mode over Fast Mode?
- A) When you need the agent to perform quick terminal executions without pausing for reviews.
- B) When you are working on simple code fixes that need to be committed in under 10 seconds.
- C) When you are executing complex, multi-step tasks that require a structured design plan, artifact outputs, and human-in-the-loop review.
Feedback: Option C is correct. Planning Mode allows the agent to reason, group tasks, and create structured plans for complex or high-risk modifications.
The High-Velocity Terminal Surface

The agy command line interface is a lightweight, terminal-first client that communicates directly with the local execution engine. With a zero-GUI footprint, it is designed for developers, systems administrators, and DevOps pipelines. It enables high-speed workspace validation, codebase querying, and batch task automation without the overhead of rendering graphical windows or web viewports.
Core Command Syntax and Usage
The agy CLI operates within the context of the user's active directory. Security policies, directory lockouts, and credentials configured for the workspace are automatically respected.

# Run system and environment diagnostics to verify configuration parity
agy --verify

# Execute a semantic query across files in the active directory
agy query "Locate all files that set environment variables or establish API endpoints"

# Dispatch a direct modification task to the agent
agy run --task "Find all script files in ./bin, change permissions to executable, and document dependencies"
Shell Integration and Pipeline Piping
Because the CLI outputs structured text, it integrates seamlessly with standard Unix utilities (like find, grep, and xargs) to construct automated development loops.

# Find modified code files and pipe them directly into agy for automated review
find ./src -name "*.py" -mtime -1 | xargs agy run --task "Audit these files for credentials or syntax violations"

Safety Warning: Running tasks using the --auto-approve flag executes all file operations, terminal command suggestions, and network tools without pausing for human permission. To prevent accidental host directory corruption or data leaks, always run auto-approve tasks inside a dedicated, containerized sandbox (agy-box).
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: Which agy command is used to ensure the local workstation has valid credentials and complies with active workspace rules?
- A) agy run --task
- B) agy --verify
- C) agy query --status
Feedback: Option B is correct. The --verify command validates system prerequisites, checks active cloud tokens, and ensures the workspace lock state is sound.
Question 2: What is the recommended safety measure when using the --auto-approve flag on agy commands?
- A) Run the command with root user permissions on the host OS.
- B) Execute the command exclusively inside an isolated container sandbox like agy-box.
- C) Disable local workspace folder locking rules.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Auto-approval runs commands without pausing for human reviews; running inside a container sandbox protects host directories from unintended modifications.
Agent Manager: Asynchronous Mission Control

The antigravity standalone application serves as a visual Agent Manager. Traditional chatbot interfaces operate linearly and synchronously—requiring the developer to wait for one answer before posing the next. The Agent Manager bypasses this bottleneck by serving as an asynchronous Mission Control. Developers define high-level tasks, launch parallel agent instances across different project directories, and monitor multiple work streams simultaneously.
Mission Cards, Lanes, and Artifact Lists
Tasks are managed visually using 'Mission Cards' organized in status lanes (Queued, Running, Completed, and Failed):

items: Queued: The task is initialized and waits for execution tokens or backend workspace authorization.
Running: The agent is active. Clicking the card opens a live side drawer displaying execution logs, model details, and terminal command streams.
Completed: The task finished. The card displays direct links to the generated documents, code files, and verification walkthroughs.
Failed: The task encountered a compilation error, boundary block, or budget exhaustion. Detailed error logs and stacks are displayed directly on the card.

blocks.content: As the agent proceeds, it lists its produced Artifacts directly on the card. This includes Task Lists, Implementation Plans, and Walkthroughs, which the developer can inspect, review, and comment on.
Model Configurations and Mode Selections
When creating a new Mission Card, the user configures two parameters:

items: Model Selection: Choose which model (e.g. Gemini 3 Pro) the agent will use for execution.
Mode Selection: Toggle between Fast (for simple terminal actions and quick changes) or Planning (for complex tasks requiring task group design and plans).

blocks.content: Step-by-Step Lab: Orchestrating a Parallel App Build
This lab guides you through dispatching an asynchronous task to build a simple FastAPI application:

items: 1. Launch the antigravity standalone application and click 'Open Folder' to select your workspace folder.
2. Ensure the Mode Selection is set to 'Planning' in the side panel.
3. Submit the prompt: 'Create a todo list web application using Python FastAPI.'
4. Observe the agent analyzing the request and producing an Implementation Plan. Under 'Review policy', review the plan details.
5. Add a Google Docs-style comment on the plan: 'Please use SQLite for data storage and ensure the models are documented.' Submit the comment.
6. Observe the agent re-planning, updating its Task List, and generating requirements.txt, main.py, and templates.
7. Click 'Proceed' or 'Accept' to allow the agent to finalize the build and watch it verify the server.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: How does the Antigravity Agent Manager resolve the linear limitation of traditional AI chat tools?
- A) By translating code into natural language documentation.
- B) By allowing developers to dispatch multiple agent instances simultaneously to work asynchronously on parallel task cards.
- C) By compiling code instantly without requiring local runtime setups.
Feedback: Option B is correct. The Agent Manager is an asynchronous mission control center that can manage multiple parallel agents operating in different lanes.
Question 2: What happens when you add a comment to a generated Implementation Plan in the visual card?
- A) The agent ignores the comment and continues executing the original plan.
- B) The comment is submitted to the engine, prompting the agent to update its design and re-plan its task list before executing code.
- C) The task card fails immediately and moves to the Failed lane.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Google Docs-style feedback on artifacts like plans and tasks prompts the agent to iterate on its approach before writing code.
Synchronous Co-Authoring & Editor Surface

The workspace editor, antigravity-ide, is a customized fork of VS Code. While the visual board focuses on high-level asynchronous orchestration, the Editor is designed for synchronous, side-by-side co-authoring. It integrates agent capabilities directly into your editing workflows, ensuring that developer muscle memory for syntax styling, extensions, and file trees is fully preserved.
Active Editor Assistant Capabilities
As you type or modify code, the agent-driven editor provides three core assisting behaviors:

items: Auto-complete: Context-aware suggestions appear inline as gray text; press Tab to accept.
Tab to Import: The editor detects missing dependencies or libraries and suggests importing them automatically.
Tab to Jump: Moves the cursor to the next logical location (such as block endings or argument spots) to maintain writing speed.

blocks.content: Interactive Agent Panels and Context Commands
The Editor interface offers several natural ways to collaborate with the agent:

items: Inline Commands (Cmd+I / Ctrl+I): Highlight a block of code or text in the editor or terminal and prompt the agent to modify, refactor, or explain it. Changes are shown as an inline green-and-red diff overlay.
Agent Side Panel (Cmd+L / Ctrl+L): Open the persistent agent chat panel to ask questions. Reference specific files, directories, or tools using '@'.
Explain and Fix: Hover over code diagnostics or build warnings, click 'Explain and Fix', and the agent automatically attempts to rewrite the line.
Send to Agent: Select compiler outputs, terminal errors, or test failures, and send them directly to the active agent context for diagnosis.

blocks.content: Workspace Customizations: Rules and Workflows
Developers can customize agent behavior locally using two distinct concepts:

items: Rules: Workspace-wide guidelines (saved under .agents/rules/) that act as permanent system instructions. The agent reads these to ensure all output complies with styling (e.g. PEP 8) or architectural principles.
Workflows: User-defined prompts (saved under .agents/workflows/) that can be triggered on demand using '/' inside the chat side panel.

blocks.content: Step-by-Step Lab: Implementing Rules and Workflows
This lab shows how to implement custom rules and workflows to build unit tests:

items: 1. Open antigravity-ide and select your active project directory.
2. Under Customizations, click 'Rules' and create a workspace rule named 'pep8-strict' to enforce PEP 8 guidelines and commenting on all files.
3. Create a workspace workflow named 'generate-unit-tests' with the instruction to generate unit tests prefixed with test_ for each file and method.
4. Create a main.py file and ask the agent (Cmd+I): 'Implement bubble sort and binary search.'
5. Review the inline diff overlay. Observe that the agent split the logic into separate modular files (bubble_sort.py, binary_search.py) matching the active rules. Click Accept.
6. In the Agent Side Panel, type '/generate-unit-tests' and submit. Verify that test_bubble_sort.py and test_binary_search.py are generated.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the purpose of the inline diff overlay in the Editor when using Cmd+I?
- A) To compile the active file in the background.
- B) To show proposed agent changes as red deletions and green additions directly in the editor tab before they are accepted.
- C) To trace memory leaks in the active application.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Inline diff overlays allow developers to audit exactly what code the agent plans to modify or add before committing to the file.
Question 2: What is the main distinction between Rules and Workflows in workspace customization?
- A) Rules are saved globally, while Workflows can only be saved in the workspace.
- B) Rules act as persistent system instructions that the agent always follows, while Workflows are custom prompts triggered on demand using the '/' key.
- C) Rules control security bounds, while Workflows handle third-party library imports.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Rules permanently guide style and architecture during all agent actions, whereas Workflows are on-demand shortcuts for specific tasks.
The Managed Browser Surface & Subagents

Google Antigravity features a managed Browser surface that allows agents to interact with web pages. When the main agent encounters a web search, API documentation research, or app testing task, it spawns a specialized 'Browser Subagent'. This subagent runs a model optimized for web page operations. It has access to browser-level tools to click elements, scroll sections, type inputs, read console logs, and navigate URLs.
Browser Safety and JavaScript Policies
Operating on live web pages presents security risks. Antigravity mitigates this by enforcing three strict policies controlled in the application settings:

items: Always Proceed: The subagent runs JavaScript on pages without pausing. This gives the agent maximum autonomy but exposes the system to active web exploits.
Request Review: The subagent pauses and requests manual human approval before executing any JavaScript on the target page.
Disabled: JavaScript execution is completely blocked within the browser subagent.

blocks.content: Closing the 'Trust Gap' with Browser Artifacts

When an agent claims it has verified an application, developers face a 'Trust Gap'—the need to manually reproduce the steps to be sure. Antigravity closes this gap by outputting visual proof directly to the workspace as artifacts:

items: DOM Captures: Structured markdown parses of the page's HTML structure.
Screenshots: Full-resolution static captures of the page at critical states.

Playback Tapes (Browser Recordings): Video recordings of the browser session (saved under .gemini/recordings/). Developers can watch the exact interaction flow (e.g. clicking logs, seeing validation spinners) to audit functional requirements.

blocks.content: Cross-Interface Data Sync & Interoperability

Although the CLI client (agy), the standalone Agent Manager (antigravity), and the Editor (antigravity-ide) are separate interfaces, they operate on a single shared source of truth. Under the hood, they read and write to the same project directory. The shared state is maintained through:

items: The workspace database cache (.gemini/ folder) which stores logs, history, and subagent playback recordings.
Workspace rules (.gemini.md or .agents/rules/) which enforce identical file-system firewalls and access permissions across all three interfaces.
File-system updates: Files scraped or generated by an agent running in the Visual Board appear instantly in the Editor explorer and can be verified via the CLI.

blocks.content: Coordinated Scenario: A developer launches a research card in the Visual Board to scrape API data to api_ref.json. Next, they run 'agy --verify' in the terminal to confirm the file is valid. Finally, they open antigravity-ide and use the sidecar to generate python calls referencing that file.

Concurrency Warning: Avoid triggering concurrent write operations on the same file from different interfaces (e.g., executing a command pipeline in agy that modifies a file while editing it in the IDE). This can bypass local locks and result in merge conflicts.
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the primary role of the Browser Subagent in Google Antigravity?
- A) To compile typescript files into HTML files.
- B) To run a specialized model that controls an integrated browser session to navigate, click, scroll, and extract content.
- C) To route API calls through a proxy server.
Feedback: Option B is correct. The Browser Subagent is a specialized helper that executes browsing actions like DOM capture, clicks, and page scrolls using a model optimized for web page operations.
Question 2: How does Antigravity close the 'Trust Gap' when an agent verifies a dynamic web interface?
- A) By encrypting code edits so they cannot be modified.
- B) By generating visual artifacts like screenshots and Playback Tape videos of the automated browser session.
- C) By requiring the developer to execute manual selenium tests.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Browser recordings and screenshots provide verifiable proof of page state and functional validation, resolving the Trust Gap.
